id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_0
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: [Page 1] FIELD EMERGENCY CARE GUIDE For Use in Conflict Zones No Medical Equipment Assumed Designed for civilians with no medical training REFERENCES: TCCC Guidelines | WHO Emergency Care | Wilderness & Battlefield Medicine CONDITIONS COVERED IN THIS DOCUMENT 1. Post-Pregnancy Emergencies (Mother & Baby) 2. Shrapnel / Shard Injuries 3. Gunshot Wounds 4. Blast Injuries / Explosion 5. Airway & Breathing Emergencies THIS DOCUMENT IS FOR EMERGENCY REFERENCE ONLY. Seek professional medical care as soon as it becomes available. Content is based on established field medicine protocols and does not replace trained medical personnel. [Page 2] How to Use This Guide This guide is written for people with NO medical training who must act in an emergency when no doctor or hospital is available. Every section follows the same structure: Section What it tells you Overview What the condition is and why it is dangerous Signs & Symptoms How to recognize the condition Immediate Actions What to do in the first minutes step by step Ongoing Care What to keep doing until help arrives What NOT to Do Common mistakes that make things worse When to Move Whether and how to transport the person Reference Source of the protocol ALWAYS ensure your own safety first. Do not enter an unsecured area, active fire zone, or building at risk of collapse to help someone. The Universal First Check Do This For Every Patient 1. Is the scene safe? If not, wait or move the person to cover. 2. Is the person conscious? Shout their name, tap their shoulder. 3. Are they breathing? Watch chest rise for 10 seconds. 4. Is there severe bleeding? Look for blood soaking clothing rapidly. 5. Then go to the specific condition section below. [Page 3] 1. Post-Pregnancy Emergencies Source: WHO Guidelines for the Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage and Retained Placenta (2009); Mdecins Sans Frontires (MSF) Obstetrics in Remote Settings; UNHCR Reproductive Health in Refugee Situations. 1A. Postpartum Hemorrhage (Severe Bleeding After Birth) Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) is the leading cause of maternal death worldwide. It occurs when the mother bleeds heavily after delivering the baby. Heavy bleeding can kill within 2 hours if not controlled. SIGNS & SYMPTOMS Soaking more than one large cloth or pad per 15 minutes Blood pooling rapidly beneath the mother Mother feels faint, dizzy, or confused Pale, cold, clammy skin Rapid, weak pulse (heart beating very fast but faintly) Uterus (belly

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_1
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: bleeding can kill within 2 hours if not controlled. SIGNS & SYMPTOMS Soaking more than one large cloth or pad per 15 minutes Blood pooling rapidly beneath the mother Mother feels faint, dizzy, or confused Pale, cold, clammy skin Rapid, weak pulse (heart beating very fast but faintly) Uterus (belly below navel) feels soft and floppy instead of firm IMMEDIATE ACTIONS DO THESE IN ORDER 6. MASSAGE THE UTERUS: Place one hand flat on the lower belly, just below the navel. Press down firmly and rub in a circular motion. The uterus should harden like a ball under your hand within 12 minutes. This is the single most important action. Continue until it stays firm. 7. PUT THE BABY TO BREAST: If the baby is able, place it to the mother's breast to suckle immediately. Breastfeeding causes the body to release hormones that contract the uterus and reduce bleeding. Do this even while massaging. 8. CONTROL EXTERNAL BLEEDING: Pack clean cloths tightly against any visible bleeding wound from delivery tears. Apply direct firm pressure. Do not remove cloths add more on top if soaked. 9. KEEP HER WARM: Cover the mother with any available cloth or blanket. Cold worsens bleeding. 10. ELEVATE HER LEGS: If she is dizzy or unresponsive, lay her flat and raise her legs above the level of her heart using rolled clothing or bags. This pushes blood toward vital organs. 11. DO NOT LEAVE HER ALONE: Stay with her and monitor continuously. If bleeding does not slow within 15 minutes of uterus massage, she is in severe danger. Begin evacuation to any medical facility immediately while continuing care. [Page 4] Ongoing Care Continue uterine massage every 510 minutes to ensure the uterus stays firm Encourage oral fluids (water, broth) if she is conscious and not vomiting Track her pulse: press two fingers on the inside of her wrist. More than 100 beats per minute is alarming. More than 120 is critical. Keep her calm and reassured fear increases blood loss What NOT to Do Do NOT pull on the umbilical cord to deliver the placenta. This can cause life- threatening hemorrhage and uterine inversion. Do NOT give the mother alcohol. Do NOT leave the placenta inside without medical help beyond 1 hour post- delivery. 1B. Eclampsia (Seizures During or After Pregnancy) Eclampsia is a dangerous condition where a pregnant or recently delivered woman has seizures due

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_2
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: can cause life- threatening hemorrhage and uterine inversion. Do NOT give the mother alcohol. Do NOT leave the placenta inside without medical help beyond 1 hour post- delivery. 1B. Eclampsia (Seizures During or After Pregnancy) Eclampsia is a dangerous condition where a pregnant or recently delivered woman has seizures due to very high blood pressure. It can occur up to 6 weeks after delivery. SIGNS & SYMPTOMS Sudden seizure (shaking, convulsions, loss of consciousness) Before the seizure: severe headache, blurred vision, swelling of face/hands She may be unresponsive after the seizure for several minutes Confusion or unusual behavior after a seizure IMMEDIATE ACTIONS 12. PROTECT HER FROM INJURY: Clear the area around her of hard objects. Do not hold her down. Do not put anything in her mouth. 13. POSITION: Turn her gently onto her left side during and after the seizure (recovery position). This keeps her airway clear and reduces pressure on major blood vessels. 14. TIME THE SEIZURE: Note when it starts. A seizure lasting more than 5 minutes is a medical emergency. 15. AFTER THE SEIZURE: Keep her on her left side. Ensure she is breathing. Talk to her calmly as she regains consciousness. 16. EVACUATE IMMEDIATELY: Eclampsia requires urgent hospital care. This is a life- threatening condition. [Page 5] Do NOT put fingers or objects in her mouth. You will be bitten and it does not help her. 1C. Newborn Not Breathing at Birth Most babies who do not cry immediately after birth can be resuscitated with basic stimulation. Reference: WHO Basic Newborn Resuscitation (2012). IMMEDIATE ACTIONS FIRST 60 SECONDS ARE CRITICAL 17. DRY AND WARM: Immediately wrap the baby in a dry cloth. Rub the back and soles of feet vigorously for 30 seconds. This stimulates breathing. 18. CLEAR THE AIRWAY: Wipe the mouth and nose with a clean cloth. Do not suction unless you have a bulb syringe. 19. POSITION: Lay the baby flat on its back with the head in a neutral position (not tilted back, not chin on chest). Place a small roll under the shoulders. 20. CHECK BREATHING: Watch for chest rise. Listen near the mouth. If breathing: keep warm, give to mother for skin-to-skin contact. If NOT breathing after 30 seconds of stimulation: begin rescue breaths. 21. RESCUE BREATHS: Cover both the baby's mouth AND nose with your mouth. Give 5 small, gentle puffs just enough to see the chest

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_3
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: rise. Listen near the mouth. If breathing: keep warm, give to mother for skin-to-skin contact. If NOT breathing after 30 seconds of stimulation: begin rescue breaths. 21. RESCUE BREATHS: Cover both the baby's mouth AND nose with your mouth. Give 5 small, gentle puffs just enough to see the chest rise slightly. Then give 1 breath every 3 seconds (20 per minute). Continue for 2 minutes. Check again for breathing. 22. IF NO PULSE: If the baby is not breathing and you cannot feel a heartbeat by pressing 2 fingers on the center of the chest, begin CPR: 3 chest compressions (pressing down about 1.5 cm with 2 fingers on the breastbone) followed by 1 breath. Repeat continuously. A baby that is limp and blue but warm still has a chance. Keep going. Babies can be resuscitated for several minutes after birth. 1D. Newborn Hypothermia (Baby Getting Too Cold) Newborns lose heat extremely rapidly, especially in cold or damp environments. Hypothermia is a leading preventable cause of newborn death in field conditions. SIGNS THE BABY IS TOO COLD IF: Skin is cold to touch on belly or back Lethargic, not feeding, not crying Blue or purple lips and fingernails [Page 6] Breathing slowly or weakly IMMEDIATE ACTIONS 23. SKIN-TO-SKIN: Place the baby directly on the mother's bare chest, between her breasts. Cover both with a blanket. This is called kangaroo care and is the most effective rewarming method available without equipment. 24. COVER THE HEAD: The baby loses most heat through the head. Wrap or cover it immediately. 25. KEEP ENVIRONMENT WARM: Move away from wind, rain, or cold surfaces. Use body heat from any available adult if the mother is unable. 26. FEED: If baby can suckle, breastfeed immediately. Feeding provides calories and warmth. 27. REASSESS: After 30 minutes of skin-to-skin contact, the baby should be warmer, pinker, and more active. 1E. Retained Placenta (Placenta Not Delivered) Source: WHO Guidelines for the Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage and Retained Placenta (2009); MSF Obstetrics in Remote Settings. After the baby is born, the placenta (afterbirth) should normally deliver within 30 minutes. If it does not come out, it is called a retained placenta. This is a leading cause of postpartum hemorrhage and requires urgent care. SIGNS Placenta has not delivered 3060 minutes after the baby was born Continued moderate bleeding despite a firm uterus Mother feels continued cramping or fullness in

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_4
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: If it does not come out, it is called a retained placenta. This is a leading cause of postpartum hemorrhage and requires urgent care. SIGNS Placenta has not delivered 3060 minutes after the baby was born Continued moderate bleeding despite a firm uterus Mother feels continued cramping or fullness in the lower belly IMMEDIATE ACTIONS 28. DO NOT PULL THE CORD: Never pull, tug, or yank the umbilical cord to try to remove the placenta. This is the most dangerous mistake a bystander can make. It can cause uterine inversion (see 1F) and massive fatal hemorrhage. 29. CONTINUE UTERUS MASSAGE: Keep massaging the lower belly to keep the uterus contracted and minimize bleeding. 30. ENCOURAGE BREASTFEEDING: Put the baby to breast. Natural hormones released during suckling help the uterus contract and may help expel the placenta. 31. KEEP HER UPRIGHT: If able, help her squat or sit upright gravity may assist. [Page 7] 32. MONITOR BLEEDING: If bleeding increases significantly, treat as postpartum hemorrhage (see 1A). 33. EVACUATE: Retained placenta beyond 60 minutes requires manual removal by a trained provider. Begin evacuation while continuing care. Do NOT attempt to manually remove the placenta without medical training. Do NOT pull the cord under any circumstances. 1F. Uterine Inversion (Uterus Turned Inside Out) Source: ICRC War Surgery; MSF Emergency Obstetric Care. Uterine inversion is a rare but immediately life-threatening emergency, most commonly caused by pulling the umbilical cord too hard. The uterus partially or completely turns inside out through the vaginal opening. SIGNS RECOGNIZE IMMEDIATELY Sudden massive bleeding immediately after delivery A dark red mass visible at or protruding from the vaginal opening Mother goes into shock almost instantly pale, collapsed, unresponsive Severe pain OR no pain at all (if the mother is already in shock) FIELD ACTIONS 34. DO NOT ATTEMPT TO PUSH IT BACK IN: Without training and sterile conditions, attempting to replace the uterus causes further injury and infection. Leave it as it is. 35. COVER WITH CLEAN DAMP CLOTH: Place the cleanest available damp cloth gently over the exposed tissue to protect it from drying out and contamination. 36. LAY HER FLAT: Elevate her legs. She will be in severe shock. 37. KEEP WARM: Cover her completely. 38. EVACUATE IMMEDIATELY: This is a surgical emergency. She will die without intervention. This is the highest evacuation priority. Uterine inversion is caused by pulling the umbilical cord. This is why

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_5
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: 36. LAY HER FLAT: Elevate her legs. She will be in severe shock. 37. KEEP WARM: Cover her completely. 38. EVACUATE IMMEDIATELY: This is a surgical emergency. She will die without intervention. This is the highest evacuation priority. Uterine inversion is caused by pulling the umbilical cord. This is why cord traction must never be performed by untrained persons. 1G. Birth Canal Tears (Vaginal and Perineal Lacerations) Source: WHO Managing Complications in Pregnancy and Childbirth (2017). [Page 8] Tears to the vaginal opening and perineum (area between vagina and anus) are very common after delivery, especially first deliveries. They cause continuous bleeding that is separate from uterine bleeding. SIGNS Continuous slow or moderate bleeding from below, even when the uterus feels firm and contracted Blood flows steadily rather than in gushes Visible wound or tear around the vaginal opening Mother reports sharp pain in the perineal area IMMEDIATE ACTIONS 39. APPLY DIRECT PRESSURE: Place a clean folded cloth firmly against the bleeding area and hold steady pressure. Ask the mother to squeeze her thighs together if able to help maintain pressure. 40. HOLD FOR 10 MINUTES: Do not lift to check maintain continuous pressure for a full 10 minutes. 41. IF DEEP TEAR: If the wound is deep and pressure alone does not control it, gently pack clean cloth into the vaginal opening and maintain external pressure on top. This is a last resort. 42. TREAT FOR BLOOD LOSS: Encourage fluids if conscious. Monitor pulse. 43. EVACUATE: Lacerations need suturing to fully stop bleeding and prevent infection. Distinguish tear bleeding from uterine bleeding: press on the uterus. If the uterus is firm but bleeding continues, the source is a tear, not the uterus. Both can occur simultaneously. 1H. Newborn Infection (Neonatal Sepsis) Source: WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children; UNHCR Newborn Health in Humanitarian Settings. Newborn infection (sepsis) is one of the leading causes of neonatal death worldwide. It develops in the first hours to days after birth, particularly after delivery in non-sterile conditions. It can kill within 2448 hours if untreated. SIGNS WATCH FOR THESE IN FIRST 3 DAYS [Page 9] Baby refuses to feed or feeds very weakly when previously feeding well Fever: baby feels hot to touch on the belly or back (OR is abnormally cold both are danger signs) Weak, high-pitched, or unusual cry or no cry at all Belly looks swollen or hard

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_6
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: 3 DAYS [Page 9] Baby refuses to feed or feeds very weakly when previously feeding well Fever: baby feels hot to touch on the belly or back (OR is abnormally cold both are danger signs) Weak, high-pitched, or unusual cry or no cry at all Belly looks swollen or hard Skin turns yellow (jaundice) in first 24 hours abnormal, not normal newborn jaundice Rapid breathing (more than 60 breaths per minute) or grunting sounds Baby is limp, floppy, not moving normally Redness, swelling, or discharge around the umbilical cord stump FIELD CARE 44. KEEP WARM: Skin-to-skin contact with the mother is the best way to maintain safe temperature. Septic babies lose heat fast. 45. BREASTFEED: Breast milk contains antibodies that fight infection. Attempt breastfeeding every 12 hours. If the baby is too weak to suck, try expressing milk and giving it by spoon or clean finger. 46. KEEP THE CORD CLEAN: Do not apply anything to the cord stump except letting it air dry. If the cord is infected (red, smelly, discharging pus), cover loosely with clean dry cloth. 47. DO NOT GIVE HERBAL REMEDIES OR LOCAL TREATMENTS to a sick newborn. 48. EVACUATE URGENTLY: Neonatal sepsis requires intravenous antibiotics. There is no effective field treatment. Every hour matters. A baby that stops feeding is always a medical emergency. Do not wait to see if it improves on its own. 1I. Umbilical Cord Problems Source: WHO Basic Newborn Care Guidelines; MSF Neonatal Protocols. Cord Bleeding After Cutting After the cord is cut and tied, the cut end should stop bleeding quickly. If it continues to bleed, the tie was not tight enough. 49. RE-TIE the cord tightly with clean string, cloth strip, or shoelace as close to the baby's belly as possible without crushing the skin. 50. If you have no tie: pinch the cord end between fingers and hold for 5 minutes. 51. A small amount of oozing is normal. Active dripping or flowing is not. Cord Around the Baby's Neck During Birth (Nuchal Cord) [Page 10] Finding the umbilical cord wrapped around the baby's neck during delivery is common and usually manageable. 52. FEEL for the cord around the neck as the baby's head delivers run one finger around the neck. 53. If the cord is LOOSE: gently slip it over the baby's head or over the baby's body as it delivers. Do this calmly and without pulling.

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_7
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: common and usually manageable. 52. FEEL for the cord around the neck as the baby's head delivers run one finger around the neck. 53. If the cord is LOOSE: gently slip it over the baby's head or over the baby's body as it delivers. Do this calmly and without pulling. 54. If the cord is TIGHT and cannot be slipped free: DO NOT FORCE IT. Continue with the delivery. Guide the baby's body out in the normal direction the cord will stretch. Do not pull or cut the cord at this stage. 55. After full delivery: manage cord bleeding as above. Never cut a cord that is tightly around the neck before the baby is fully delivered this removes the baby's oxygen supply before it can breathe on its own. 1J. Premature Baby (Born Too Early) Source: WHO Kangaroo Mother Care: A Practical Guide (2003); MSF Neonatal Care in Resource-Limited Settings. A baby born before 37 weeks of pregnancy is premature. Premature babies are smaller, have thinner skin, and lose heat many times faster than full-term babies. Hypothermia is the primary killer of premature infants in field conditions. RECOGNIZING A PREMATURE BABY Smaller and lighter than expected Thin, almost transparent or red skin Little body fat ribs may be visible Head appears proportionally larger Less active, weaker cry, weaker sucking reflex May have difficulty breathing rapid breathing, nostrils flaring, skin pulling in at ribs IMMEDIATE ACTIONS WARMTH IS SURVIVAL 56. DRY IMMEDIATELY: Dry the baby vigorously and discard the wet cloth. Do not leave the baby on any wet surface even for a moment. 57. SKIN-TO-SKIN WITHOUT INTERRUPTION: Place the baby directly on the mother's bare chest in an upright position between her breasts. Cover both completely head included with warm dry cloth. This is called Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC) and is the single most effective intervention available without equipment. [Page 11] 58. KEEP SKIN-TO-SKIN CONTINUOUSLY: Unlike a full-term baby, a premature baby must remain in skin-to-skin contact at all times 24 hours a day until it is larger and stronger. Even brief removal causes dangerous cooling. 59. FEED FREQUENTLY: Attempt breastfeeding every 12 hours. If the baby cannot latch due to weakness, express milk and give by clean spoon or dropper. A premature baby that is not fed will deteriorate rapidly. 60. MONITOR BREATHING: Count breaths. Normal for premature newborn is 4060 per minute. Over 60 with visible effort

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_8
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: Attempt breastfeeding every 12 hours. If the baby cannot latch due to weakness, express milk and give by clean spoon or dropper. A premature baby that is not fed will deteriorate rapidly. 60. MONITOR BREATHING: Count breaths. Normal for premature newborn is 4060 per minute. Over 60 with visible effort is a danger sign. 61. EVACUATE: Premature babies under 34 weeks gestation require specialist care. Begin evacuation while maintaining kangaroo care it can continue during transport. Kangaroo Mother Care is endorsed by WHO as equal to incubator care in stable premature infants. It requires nothing except warmth and proximity. Any adult (not just the mother) can provide it if the mother is unable. 1K. Maternal Fever and Infection After Birth (Puerperal Sepsis) Source: WHO Managing Complications in Pregnancy and Childbirth (2017). Infection of the uterus or birth canal after delivery (puerperal sepsis) is a leading cause of maternal death. It typically appears 24 hours to several days after birth. SIGNS Fever (body feels hot, especially after the first 24 hours post-delivery) Foul-smelling discharge from the vagina Increasing belly pain and tenderness General weakness, chills, rapid pulse Mother appears increasingly unwell rather than recovering FIELD CARE 62. Keep her hydrated encourage water and fluids constantly if she is not vomiting. 63. Keep her warm but do not overheat. 64. Continue breastfeeding infection does not pass to the baby through breast milk in most cases. 65. Do NOT apply anything inside the vagina. 66. EVACUATE URGENTLY: Puerperal sepsis requires antibiotics. Without treatment it progresses to septic shock and death within days. [Page 12] 2. Shrapnel & Shard Injuries Source: Tactical Combat Casualty Care (TCCC) Guidelines, Committee on TCCC (2023); International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) War Surgery, Volume 1. Shrapnel injuries result from fragments metal, glass, bone, or debris entering the body at high velocity. A single explosion can cause dozens of entry wounds. The most immediately life- threatening are wounds that cause severe bleeding or penetrate the chest or abdomen. 2A. Assess the Injury Do This First Zone Priority Head / Neck CRITICAL risk of airway loss, major vessel bleeding Chest (front or back) CRITICAL risk of collapsed lung, heart tamponade Abdomen / groin CRITICAL internal bleeding likely Arms and legs HIGH control bleeding first Small embedded fragments in skin LOW do not remove in the field 2B. Controlling Life-Threatening Bleeding from Shrapnel IMMEDIATE ACTIONS 67. EXPOSE: Cut away or

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_9
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: Chest (front or back) CRITICAL risk of collapsed lung, heart tamponade Abdomen / groin CRITICAL internal bleeding likely Arms and legs HIGH control bleeding first Small embedded fragments in skin LOW do not remove in the field 2B. Controlling Life-Threatening Bleeding from Shrapnel IMMEDIATE ACTIONS 67. EXPOSE: Cut away or remove clothing from around the wound to see it clearly. 68. DIRECT PRESSURE: Press the cleanest material available (clothing, clean cloth) firmly and directly over the wound. Use your palm or your fist for deep wounds. 69. PACK THE WOUND: If the wound is deep or a hole (not a surface cut), push clean cloth INTO the wound with your finger. Pack tightly. This is called wound packing. Then press hard on top. 70. MAINTAIN PRESSURE: Hold firm pressure for a minimum of 10 minutes without lifting. Do not peek. If blood soaks through, add more material on top do not remove the first layer. 71. TOURNIQUET FOR LIMB WOUNDS: If bleeding is from an arm or leg and cannot be controlled with pressure, apply a tourniquet (see tourniquet instructions below). 72. DO NOT REMOVE EMBEDDED OBJECTS: If a large piece of shrapnel, glass, or metal is embedded in the body, do NOT pull it out. It may be plugging a vessel. Stabilize it in place by padding around it. [Page 13] 2C. Improvised Tourniquet Limbs Only A tourniquet stops blood flow to a limb. Use it only when direct pressure fails and the wound is on an arm or leg. It will cause pain but saves lives. 73. Use a strip of cloth at least 4 cm (2 inches) wide a torn shirt sleeve, belt, or scarf. 74. Wrap it around the limb 58 cm (23 inches) ABOVE the wound (closer to the body, not below it). 75. Tie a half-knot, place a stick or pen on the knot, tie another knot over the stick. 76. Twist the stick until bleeding stops. The person will feel severe pain this is expected. 77. Secure the stick so it cannot unwind. Tie it in place. 78. Write the time applied on the person's forehead or arm with anything available. Note it if you can. 79. Do NOT remove the tourniquet. Only a medical professional should remove it. A tourniquet left on longer than 2 hours increases risk of limb loss. Evacuation to medical care is urgent. Write the time applied. 2D.

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_10
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: the person's forehead or arm with anything available. Note it if you can. 79. Do NOT remove the tourniquet. Only a medical professional should remove it. A tourniquet left on longer than 2 hours increases risk of limb loss. Evacuation to medical care is urgent. Write the time applied. 2D. Chest Wound from Shrapnel (Sucking Chest Wound) A wound that penetrates the chest wall allows air to enter the chest cavity, collapsing the lung. You may hear a sucking or hissing sound with each breath. SIGNS Wound in the chest that makes a sucking or bubbling sound when breathing Breathing becomes increasingly difficult and rapid Lips or fingernails turn blue Victim becomes agitated, then unresponsive IMMEDIATE ACTIONS 80. SEAL THE WOUND: Place the cleanest material available (plastic bag, plastic wrapper, tape) over the wound. Tape 3 sides only leave one side open to act as a valve. If no tape: hold the seal in place with your hand. 81. SIT THEM UP: Help the person sit upright or lean slightly toward the injured side. This helps the good lung work better. 82. MONITOR: If the person suddenly gets much worse (extreme breathing difficulty, veins in neck bulging, trachea shifting to one side), carefully lift the seal for 12 seconds to release trapped air, then re-seal. 83. EVACUATE IMMEDIATELY: A chest wound requires surgical care. [Page 14] Do NOT seal ALL four sides of a chest wound. A fully sealed wound can trap air and create a tension pneumothorax which kills faster than the open wound. 2E. Small Embedded Fragments Field Care Multiple small fragments embedded in skin (common in blast injuries) are not immediately life- threatening. In the field without sterile tools, removal attempts often cause more harm than the fragments themselves. DO NOT attempt to remove small embedded fragments without proper tools and lighting Rinse the area with clean water if available Cover loosely with any clean material to reduce infection risk Mark the locations with a pen if possible to assist later medical care Priority is always the life threats: bleeding, airway, breathing 2F. Eye Penetration by Shrapnel or Shard Source: ICRC War Surgery Volume 1; Kuhn F. et al., Ocular Traumatology, Springer (2008). Eye injuries from fragments are extremely common in blast and explosion events. Any penetrating object in the eye is a surgical emergency. The instinct to remove it is dangerous and must be suppressed. SIGNS

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_11
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: or Shard Source: ICRC War Surgery Volume 1; Kuhn F. et al., Ocular Traumatology, Springer (2008). Eye injuries from fragments are extremely common in blast and explosion events. Any penetrating object in the eye is a surgical emergency. The instinct to remove it is dangerous and must be suppressed. SIGNS Visible object (fragment, glass, metal) in or on the eye Severe eye pain, or complete absence of pain (which may indicate deep penetration) Sudden loss of vision or blurred vision in the affected eye Eye appears deformed, sunken, or fluid leaking from the eye Blood in the white part of the eye or visible beneath the surface IMMEDIATE ACTIONS 84. DO NOT REMOVE THE OBJECT: Even a small fragment may be holding eye fluid in place. Removal causes immediate loss of eye contents and permanent blindness. 85. DO NOT RUB OR PRESS THE EYE: Any pressure on a penetrated eye can force internal contents out. 86. COVER BOTH EYES: This is critical. The eyes move together if only the injured eye is covered, movement of the uncovered eye will cause the injured eye to move too, [Page 15] worsening damage. Cover both eyes with a clean cloth or cup shape (do not press on the eye itself let the covering arch over it without touching). 87. KEEP THE PERSON STILL: Ask them not to move their head. Lay them flat if possible. 88. KEEP THEM CALM: Fear and movement worsen outcome. 89. EVACUATE URGENTLY: Eye penetration requires surgical care within hours to preserve vision. Covering BOTH eyes is not optional it is medically necessary. Explain this clearly to the person. Moving one eye moves both. 2G. Neck Injuries from Shrapnel Source: TCCC Guidelines; ATLS 10th Edition Neck Trauma. The neck contains the largest blood vessels in the body (carotid arteries and jugular veins), the airway (trachea), and the spinal cord. A shrapnel wound to the neck can kill in under 2 minutes from bleeding alone. SIGNS OF DANGEROUS NECK INJURY Active bleeding from the neck even moderate bleeding here is life-threatening Expanding swelling or bruising in the neck (blood pooling under skin) Hoarse voice, difficulty speaking, or stridor (high-pitched breathing sound) Difficulty swallowing or speaking after a neck wound Bubbling or hissing from a neck wound (trachea/airway injury) IMMEDIATE ACTIONS 90. DIRECT PRESSURE ONLY: Press clean cloth firmly over the bleeding area. Use firm sustained pressure. Do not release. 91.

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_12
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: under skin) Hoarse voice, difficulty speaking, or stridor (high-pitched breathing sound) Difficulty swallowing or speaking after a neck wound Bubbling or hissing from a neck wound (trachea/airway injury) IMMEDIATE ACTIONS 90. DIRECT PRESSURE ONLY: Press clean cloth firmly over the bleeding area. Use firm sustained pressure. Do not release. 91. NEVER USE A TOURNIQUET ON THE NECK: A tourniquet around the neck stops blood flow to the brain and kills instantly. 92. DO NOT PACK AGGRESSIVELY: Deep packing of neck wounds risks compressing the airway or causing further vessel damage. Apply surface pressure only. 93. MONITOR THE AIRWAY: Watch continuously for signs of airway difficulty noisy breathing, blue lips, inability to speak. If breathing worsens, open the airway (head tilt/chin lift, see Section 5B) and be ready to give rescue breaths. 94. KEEP HEAD STILL: Avoid unnecessary movement of the neck. If spinal injury is possible (blast threw the person), keep the head and neck aligned. 95. EVACUATE IMMEDIATELY: Neck injuries are among the highest-priority evacuations. [Page 16] A neck wound that appears small can still be cutting a major vessel internally. Do not be reassured by a small external wound. Treat all neck wounds as life- threatening. 2H. Abdominal Shrapnel Injury Source: ICRC War Surgery; NATO Emergency War Surgery. Shrapnel penetrating the abdomen causes the same internal injuries as a gunshot wound to the abdomen. There is no field treatment for internal abdominal bleeding only stabilization and evacuation. SIGNS Entry wound on the abdomen, side, or lower back Belly becomes progressively hard, rigid, and painful Signs of shock: pale, sweating, rapid weak pulse, confusion Visible bowel or tissue protruding from wound FIELD CARE 96. DO NOT pack the abdominal wound deeply with cloth this worsens internal injury. 97. DO NOT push any protruding tissue back inside. 98. COVER the wound loosely with the cleanest damp material available. 99. If an object is embedded: stabilize it with padding on all sides do not remove. 100. NOTHING BY MOUTH: No water, no food surgery will be needed. 101. LAY FLAT: Allow knees to bend if more comfortable. 102. TREAT FOR SHOCK: Keep warm, elevate legs gently. 103. EVACUATE URGENTLY: Internal abdominal injuries are universally fatal without surgery. 2I. Multiple Small Wounds Triage Priority Source: TCCC Tactical Field Care Guidelines; NATO Mass Casualty Management. In blast events, a single person may have 20 or more separate fragment wounds. The natural instinct is

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_13
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: Keep warm, elevate legs gently. 103. EVACUATE URGENTLY: Internal abdominal injuries are universally fatal without surgery. 2I. Multiple Small Wounds Triage Priority Source: TCCC Tactical Field Care Guidelines; NATO Mass Casualty Management. In blast events, a single person may have 20 or more separate fragment wounds. The natural instinct is to address the most visible wound. The most visible wound is often not the most dangerous one. [Page 17] THE CORRECT APPROACH 104. EXPOSE THE WHOLE BODY: Before treating any single wound, quickly cut or remove clothing to view the entire body surface. This takes 3060 seconds and saves lives. 105. FIND THE BIGGEST BLEED FIRST: Look for the wound with the fastest active bleeding. A wound soaking cloth in seconds outranks one that is oozing slowly. 106. USE THE ZONE TABLE (Section 2A): Identify which zone the worst wound is in and prioritize accordingly. 107. TREAT LIFE THREATS IN ORDER: Massive bleeding airway breathing all other wounds. 108. MARK TREATED WOUNDS: Use pen, chalk, or mud to mark wounds that have been dressed so nothing is missed. Small wounds in critical zones (neck, chest, abdomen) are more dangerous than large wounds on the arms and legs. Location beats size. 2J. Infection Risk from Shrapnel Wounds Source: ICRC War Surgery; WHO Wound Management Guidelines. Shrapnel carries dirt, clothing fibers, and bacteria deep into tissue. All shrapnel wounds are considered contaminated. Infection typically develops 2472 hours after injury and can become life-threatening within days without antibiotics. SIGNS OF WOUND INFECTION (appearing 2472 hours later) Increasing redness spreading from the wound edges Increasing pain and swelling around the wound Warmth around the wound Pus or cloudy discharge from the wound Red streaks extending from the wound toward the body (sign of spreading infection emergency) Fever, chills, increasing weakness FIELD PREVENTION AND CARE 109. RINSE ALL WOUNDS WITH CLEAN WATER immediately after injury. Running water is best. Use as much as available irrigation reduces infection risk more than any other field measure. [Page 18] 110. COVER WITH CLEAN DRY MATERIAL: All wounds should be covered to keep them clean. 111. DO NOT CLOSE DIRTY WOUNDS: Do not tie, tape, or suture shrapnel wounds closed in the field. Trapping contamination inside causes gangrene. 112. CHANGE DRESSINGS DAILY if possible, using the cleanest available material each time. 113. IF INFECTION DEVELOPS: Evacuate. Field treatment cannot adequately treat deep wound infection. 114. RED STREAKS FROM A

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_14
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: DIRTY WOUNDS: Do not tie, tape, or suture shrapnel wounds closed in the field. Trapping contamination inside causes gangrene. 112. CHANGE DRESSINGS DAILY if possible, using the cleanest available material each time. 113. IF INFECTION DEVELOPS: Evacuate. Field treatment cannot adequately treat deep wound infection. 114. RED STREAKS FROM A WOUND: This is a medical emergency. Septicemia (blood poisoning) can develop within hours. Evacuate immediately. Do not close shrapnel wounds. Open wounds heal. Closed contaminated wounds can cause fatal gas gangrene. 2K. Fractures Caused by Shrapnel Impact Source: PHTLS 9th Edition; NATO Emergency War Surgery. High-velocity fragments carry enough energy to shatter bone even when they do not penetrate deeply. An open fracture (bone visible or wound near broken bone) is both an orthopedic and infection emergency. SIGNS OF FRACTURE Deformity limb looks bent or out of position Inability or refusal to move the limb Severe local pain and swelling Grinding or grating sensation Bone visible through the wound (open fracture highest infection risk) FIELD CARE 115. DO NOT STRAIGHTEN THE LIMB: Immobilize it in the position you find it. Forced straightening causes additional vessel and nerve damage. 116. SPLINT IN PLACE: Use any rigid material (stick, board, rolled clothing) alongside the limb and tie above and below the fracture not over it. The splint should extend beyond the joints above and below the fracture. 117. CHECK CIRCULATION: After splinting, check that fingers or toes below the fracture are still warm and can be felt. If they are cold, pale, or numb, the splint may be too tight loosen slightly. 118. COVER OPEN FRACTURES: If bone is visible, cover loosely with the cleanest damp cloth. Do not push bone back in. Treat as a wound with high infection risk. [Page 19] 119. ELEVATE THE LIMB: If possible, rest the limb elevated above heart level to reduce swelling. 120. EVACUATE: Open fractures require surgical cleaning within hours to prevent bone infection (osteomyelitis). [Page 20] 3. Gunshot Wounds Source: TCCC Guidelines (COTCCC 2023); Prehospital Trauma Life Support (PHTLS), 9th Edition; Borden Institute Emergency War Surgery Handbook. Gunshot wounds (GSW) cause damage along the bullet's path and often beyond it due to the pressure wave. Most deaths from GSW are due to bleeding. The priority is always: STOP THE BLEEDING. 3A. The MARCH Protocol Follow This Order MARCH is the standard field casualty care sequence used by military medics worldwide. Use it for

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_15
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: the bullet's path and often beyond it due to the pressure wave. Most deaths from GSW are due to bleeding. The priority is always: STOP THE BLEEDING. 3A. The MARCH Protocol Follow This Order MARCH is the standard field casualty care sequence used by military medics worldwide. Use it for every gunshot victim. Step Action M Massive Hemorrhage Stop all life-threatening bleeding first, before anything else A Airway Ensure the person can breathe; open and clear the airway R Respiration Check and treat chest injuries C Circulation Treat shock; keep them warm and horizontal H Hypothermia Prevent heat loss; cover immediately 3B. Massive Hemorrhage Stop the Bleeding 121. DIRECT PRESSURE: Press hard on the wound with the cleanest material available. Use both hands if needed. Press as hard as you physically can. 122. WOUND PACKING: For bullet holes push clean cloth deep into the wound with your fingers. Pack as much as will fit. Press on top. 123. TOURNIQUET: For arm or leg wounds with uncontrollable bleeding, apply improvised tourniquet (see Section 2C). Apply HIGH and TIGHT as close to the armpit or groin as possible. 124. JUNCTIONAL WOUNDS (groin, armpit, neck): These areas cannot be tourniqueted. Apply direct pressure with your full body weight. Do not release. Junctional wounds (where the limb meets the body groin, armpit) are extremely dangerous and cannot have a tourniquet applied. Constant manual pressure is the only field option. [Page 21] 3C. Airway Ensure They Can Breathe 125. If conscious and talking: their airway is open. Move on. 126. If unconscious: tilt the head back gently and lift the chin up. This opens the airway. 127. Sweep the mouth with a finger: remove blood, vomit, teeth, or debris. 128. Turn them on their side (recovery position) if they are breathing but unconscious, to prevent choking on blood or vomit. 3D. Head Gunshot Wound Head wounds bleed profusely even when not life-threatening. Assess consciousness immediately. Apply gentle direct pressure to scalp wounds do NOT press hard on skull wounds where the bone may be damaged If unconscious but breathing: recovery position (on side), clear airway Do NOT remove any object embedded in the skull Do NOT give anything to eat or drink Speak calmly even if they appear unconscious hearing may remain Protect from heat and cold Evacuate immediately any loss of consciousness after head GSW requires urgent care 3E. Abdominal Gunshot Wound Abdominal GSW

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_16
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: Do NOT remove any object embedded in the skull Do NOT give anything to eat or drink Speak calmly even if they appear unconscious hearing may remain Protect from heat and cold Evacuate immediately any loss of consciousness after head GSW requires urgent care 3E. Abdominal Gunshot Wound Abdominal GSW causes internal bleeding that cannot be controlled in the field. The only treatment is surgery. SIGNS OF ABDOMINAL GSW Entry wound on abdomen, back, or flank Belly becomes rigid and painful over time Intestines or tissue visible through the wound Signs of shock: pale, sweating, rapid weak pulse, confusion FIELD CARE 129. DO NOT push protruding organs back in. Cover them with the cleanest damp cloth available. 130. DO NOT remove any object from the abdomen. [Page 22] 131. Apply a large loose dressing over the wound do not pack tightly. 132. Lay the person flat. If they are more comfortable with knees bent, allow it. 133. Keep them warm. 134. Give nothing by mouth no water, no food. 135. EVACUATE URGENTLY internal abdominal bleeding is fatal without surgery. This is a transport priority. 3F. Treating Shock Shock is the body's response to losing too much blood. It is a medical emergency. Sign of Shock Severity Anxious, slightly pale, thirsty Early act now Pale, sweating, confused, rapid pulse Moderate urgent Unresponsive, barely breathing, no pulse at Severe critical wrist Field Treatment for Shock 136. Lay the person flat. Raise legs 30 cm if no spinal injury suspected. 137. Stop all bleeding. 138. Keep them warm cover with everything available. 139. If conscious and not abdominal GSW: small sips of water or oral rehydration salts. 140. Talk to them. Keep them calm. Panic worsens shock. 141. Reassess pulse every 5 minutes. [Page 23] 4. Blast Injuries / Explosion Source: TCCC Guidelines; NATO Emergency War Surgery (4th Edition); Blast Injury Research Program, US Army Medical Research. Explosions cause four distinct types of injury that can all occur in the same person simultaneously. This is what makes blast injury more complex than a single wound. Blast Injury Type What It Means Primary pressure wave Invisible wave of pressure damages lungs, eardrums, bowel, and sinuses Secondary fragments Shrapnel and debris cause penetrating wounds (see Section 2) Tertiary thrown Person is thrown through the air, causing fractures and crush injuries Quaternary burns/crush Burns, building collapse, toxic gas inhalation 4A. Immediate Scene Actions Do NOT rush

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_17
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: wave Invisible wave of pressure damages lungs, eardrums, bowel, and sinuses Secondary fragments Shrapnel and debris cause penetrating wounds (see Section 2) Tertiary thrown Person is thrown through the air, causing fractures and crush injuries Quaternary burns/crush Burns, building collapse, toxic gas inhalation 4A. Immediate Scene Actions Do NOT rush to victims immediately after an explosion. Secondary devices (second bombs placed to target rescuers) are common in conflict zones. Wait 60 seconds and scan the area before approaching. 142. ENSURE SCENE IS SAFE: Scan for fire, structural collapse risk, gas leaks (smell), and secondary threats. 143. TRIAGE IF MULTIPLE VICTIMS: Quickly walk through and identify who is walking (minor), who is on the ground but breathing (urgent), who is not moving and not breathing (critical/expectant). 144. CALL FOR HELP: Send someone to get any available medical assistance while you begin care. 4B. Primary Blast Lung Injury The pressure wave can rupture the tiny air sacs in the lungs without any external wound. This is called blast lung and may not be obvious immediately it can worsen over hours. SIGNS MAY APPEAR WITHIN MINUTES TO HOURS AFTER BLAST Coughing up blood or pink foamy sputum Shortness of breath that worsens over time [Page 24] Chest pain, especially when breathing deeply The person was close to the blast even if they appear fine Low oxygen: lips, fingernails, or gums turn blue FIELD CARE 145. Sit the person upright do not lay flat if they have breathing difficulty 146. Keep them calm and still exertion worsens blast lung 147. Monitor breathing closely. Count breaths per minute (normal is 1220) 148. If they have both blast lung AND a chest wound: treat the chest wound first (see 2D) 149. Evacuate: blast lung can become fatal within hours 4C. Blast Ear Injury (Ruptured Eardrum) The eardrum ruptures under blast pressure. This is extremely common and usually not life- threatening, but the person may be disoriented. Signs: ringing in ears, hearing loss, pain, blood from ear canal, dizziness Cover the ear loosely with clean cloth do not insert anything into the ear canal Do NOT irrigate or flush the ear A person with blast ear injury may not be able to follow verbal instructions use hand signals and touch Most ruptures heal on their own but require medical assessment to rule out deeper injury 4D. Traumatic Amputation from Blast High-order blasts can cause partial or complete

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_18
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: or flush the ear A person with blast ear injury may not be able to follow verbal instructions use hand signals and touch Most ruptures heal on their own but require medical assessment to rule out deeper injury 4D. Traumatic Amputation from Blast High-order blasts can cause partial or complete traumatic amputation of limbs. IMMEDIATE ACTIONS 150. TOURNIQUET IMMEDIATELY: Apply as high on the limb as possible before doing anything else. A traumatic amputation will bleed fatally within minutes. 151. PACK THE STUMP: If the tourniquet does not fully control bleeding, pack clean cloth tightly into the wound and press hard. 152. DO NOT try to reattach or preserve the amputated part in field conditions without ice focus entirely on keeping the person alive. 153. TREAT FOR SHOCK: Lay flat, keep warm, elevate legs. 154. EVACUATE IMMEDIATELY. [Page 25] 4E. Building Collapse / Crush Injury If a person has been trapped under rubble or a heavy object for more than 15 minutes, releasing them can trigger a dangerous reaction called crush syndrome, where toxins released from damaged muscle can cause heart failure. If a person has been trapped for more than 15 minutes under heavy weight, DO NOT move them without medical guidance if any is available. Releasing the pressure too quickly without IV fluids can cause sudden cardiac arrest. If you MUST move them: move quickly and be prepared for sudden collapse Lay them flat immediately after extraction Encourage drinking large amounts of water if conscious Look for swollen, hard, dark limbs sign of crush injury Evacuate urgently crush syndrome requires hospital treatment 4F. Burns from Blast Source: WHO Burns Management; ICRC War Surgery. Burn Severity What it looks like Minor (1st degree) Red, painful skin, like a sunburn. No blisters. Moderate (2nd degree) Blisters, very painful, wet-looking skin Severe (3rd degree) White, brown, or black leathery skin. May be painless. Field Treatment for Burns 155. COOL THE BURN: Pour cool (not ice cold) water over the burn for 1020 minutes. This is the only burn first aid that changes outcomes. 156. COVER: After cooling, cover loosely with clean dry material. Do not wrap tightly. 157. DO NOT: burst blisters, apply toothpaste, oil, butter, or any home remedy these cause infection and make burns worse. 158. DO NOT remove clothing stuck to a burn cut around it. 159. FACE BURNS: If eyebrows are singed and the person has a

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_19
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: dry material. Do not wrap tightly. 157. DO NOT: burst blisters, apply toothpaste, oil, butter, or any home remedy these cause infection and make burns worse. 158. DO NOT remove clothing stuck to a burn cut around it. 159. FACE BURNS: If eyebrows are singed and the person has a hoarse voice or difficulty swallowing, the airway may be burned internally. This is a critical emergency requiring immediate evacuation. 160. TREAT FOR SHOCK: Severe burns cause massive fluid loss and shock. [Page 26] 5. Airway & Breathing Emergencies Source: TCCC Guidelines; WHO Basic Emergency Care (BEC); American Heart Association CPR Guidelines (2020); Advanced Trauma Life Support (ATLS) 10th Edition. Airway and breathing emergencies are the most rapidly fatal conditions. Without oxygen, brain damage begins in 4 minutes and death follows within 10. These must be treated before any other injury except massive bleeding. 5A. Assess Breathing Do This First for Every Patient 161. LOOK: Is the chest rising and falling? 162. LISTEN: Put your ear near the mouth and nose. Can you hear breath sounds? 163. FEEL: Can you feel air against your cheek? Finding Action Breathing normally Go to next priority bleeding, wounds Breathing noisily (gurgling, snoring) Clear airway see 5B Breathing very fast (>30 per min) Suspect chest injury see Section 2D or 4B Not breathing Begin rescue breaths immediately see 5C Not breathing, no pulse Begin CPR see 5D 5B. Opening and Clearing the Airway If the person is unconscious: 164. HEAD TILT-CHIN LIFT: Place one hand on the forehead, two fingers under the chin. Tilt head back and lift chin. This straightens the airway. 165. If spinal injury is suspected (fall, blast threw them, neck pain mentioned): use JAW THRUST instead place fingers behind the jaw angle and push the jaw forward without tilting the head. 166. SWEEP THE MOUTH: Use your finger to clear blood, vomit, broken teeth, or debris from the mouth. Only sweep what you can see. 167. RECOVERY POSITION: If breathing has returned turn them on their side so vomit drains out and the tongue cannot block the airway. Airway Blocked by a Solid Object Conscious Adult 168. Ask: 'Are you choking?' If they cannot speak or cough, they need help. 169. BACK BLOWS: Give 5 firm blows between the shoulder blades with the heel of your hand. [Page 27] 170. ABDOMINAL THRUSTS (Heimlich): Stand behind them, place one fist above the

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_20
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: a Solid Object Conscious Adult 168. Ask: 'Are you choking?' If they cannot speak or cough, they need help. 169. BACK BLOWS: Give 5 firm blows between the shoulder blades with the heel of your hand. [Page 27] 170. ABDOMINAL THRUSTS (Heimlich): Stand behind them, place one fist above the navel below the breastbone, cover with other hand, and give 5 sharp upward thrusts. 171. Alternate 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts until the object is expelled or the person becomes unconscious. 172. If unconscious: begin CPR each time you open the airway to give a breath, look in the mouth for the object. For pregnant women or people who are very obese: use chest thrusts (same position but hands on the center of the chest) instead of abdominal thrusts. 5C. Rescue Breathing Person Not Breathing, Has Pulse 173. Tilt head back, lift chin. 174. Pinch the nose closed. 175. Take a normal breath, seal your lips completely around their mouth. 176. Breathe in gently just enough to see the chest rise (about 1 second). 177. Let the chest fall. Give 1 breath every 56 seconds (about 1012 breaths per minute). 178. Check for pulse every 2 minutes. If no pulse: begin CPR. If you cannot or will not do mouth-to-mouth: covering the face with a thin cloth allows breath to pass through while reducing direct contact. 5D. CPR No Breathing and No Pulse CPR (Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation) keeps blood and oxygen circulating when the heart has stopped. Even imperfect CPR dramatically improves survival. CPR STEPS ADULTS AND CHILDREN OVER 8 YEARS 179. POSITION: Lay the person on their back on a firm surface. 180. HEEL OF HAND: Place the heel of one hand on the center of the chest (lower half of the breastbone). Place the other hand on top and interlock fingers. 181. COMPRESS: Push straight down firmly and fast at least 56 cm (2 inches). Let the chest fully rebound after each compression. Do not lean on the chest between compressions. 182. RATE: 100120 compressions per minute. This is faster than one per second think of the rhythm of a fast song beat. 183. RATIO: Give 30 compressions, then 2 rescue breaths. Repeat continuously. 184. If you cannot or will not give rescue breaths: give continuous chest compressions only at 100120 per minute without stopping. [Page 28] 185. Continue until: the person wakes up, starts breathing, medical

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_21
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: rhythm of a fast song beat. 183. RATIO: Give 30 compressions, then 2 rescue breaths. Repeat continuously. 184. If you cannot or will not give rescue breaths: give continuous chest compressions only at 100120 per minute without stopping. [Page 28] 185. Continue until: the person wakes up, starts breathing, medical help arrives, or you are physically unable to continue. CPR for Infants (Under 1 Year) Use 2 fingers (middle and ring finger) on the center of the chest, just below the nipple line Compress about 4 cm (1.5 inches) 30 compressions to 2 gentle puffs (cover mouth AND nose together) 100120 compressions per minute CPR for Children (18 Years) Use the heel of one hand only Compress 5 cm (2 inches) 30 compressions to 2 rescue breaths CPR on a person who still has a heartbeat can cause heart problems. Only begin CPR if you are certain there is no pulse. 5E. Choking on Blood Trauma Airway In trauma, the airway is often blocked by blood rather than a foreign object. This requires different management. 186. DO NOT do abdominal thrusts there is no solid object to dislodge. 187. ROLL ON SIDE: Turn the person onto their side so blood drains out of the mouth by gravity. 188. CLEAR WITH FINGER: If a blood clot is visible in the mouth, sweep it out with your finger. 189. SUCTION: If a suction device is available, use it. Otherwise tilt and drain. 190. If a neck or jaw wound is causing airway obstruction and the person is rapidly deteriorating, push the jaw forward and hold it there this lifts the tongue off the airway. 5F. Tension Pneumothorax Air Trapped in Chest This is one of the most rapidly fatal conditions after trauma. It occurs when air builds up inside the chest cavity (from a lung wound or blast) and compresses the heart and remaining lung. It can kill in minutes. SIGNS THE PERSON IS DETERIORATING DESPITE TREATMENT [Page 29] Breathing becomes extremely difficult and rapid Neck veins bulge visibly The trachea (windpipe) shifts away from center of the neck Blood pressure collapses person becomes unresponsive This occurs after a chest wound has been sealed FIELD ACTION 191. RELEASE THE SEAL: If a chest wound has been sealed (see 2D), lift the seal for 23 seconds to allow trapped air to escape. Then re-seal. 192. POSITION: Sit upright if possible. 193. EVACUATE: This

id: field_emergency_guide_v2_chunk_22
source: field_emergency_guide_v2
text: person becomes unresponsive This occurs after a chest wound has been sealed FIELD ACTION 191. RELEASE THE SEAL: If a chest wound has been sealed (see 2D), lift the seal for 23 seconds to allow trapped air to escape. Then re-seal. 192. POSITION: Sit upright if possible. 193. EVACUATE: This requires needle decompression (a medical procedure). There is no improvised treatment. Rapid evacuation is the only field option. [Page 30] Quick Reference Summary Use this table for rapid decision-making in the field. Condition First action Postpartum hemorrhage Uterus massage + put baby to breast immediately Eclampsia (seizure) Recovery position (left side), protect from injury, do not restrain Retained placenta Do NOT pull cord massage uterus, breastfeed, evacuate Uterine inversion Cover with damp cloth, DO NOT push back, evacuate immediately Birth canal tears Direct pressure with cloth held firmly for 10 minutes Newborn not breathing Dry and rub vigorously 30 sec, then rescue breaths Newborn too cold Skin-to-skin on mother's chest, cover head continuously Newborn infection (sepsis) Keep warm, breastfeed, evacuate urgently every hour matters Cord around neck Loose: slip over head. Tight: do not cut, continue delivery normally Premature baby Continuous skin-to-skin 24/7, cover head, feed every 12 hours Maternal fever after birth Fluids, keep warm, evacuate requires antibiotics Shrapnel severe bleeding Direct pressure, wound packing, tourniquet if limb Shrapnel chest wound 3-sided seal, sit upright Shrapnel eye penetration Cover BOTH eyes, no pressure on eye, keep still, evacuate Shrapnel neck wound Direct pressure only, NO tourniquet, monitor airway closely Shrapnel abdomen Loose cover, nothing by mouth, no deep packing, evacuate Multiple small wounds Expose full body first, find biggest bleed, treat by zone priority [Page 31] Shrapnel fracture Splint in position found, check circulation, cover open wounds Wound infection (hours later) Rinse, open cover, red streaks = evacuate immediately Gunshot any location MARCH: stop bleeding first, then airway, then breathing Gunshot abdomen Cover without packing, nothing by mouth, evacuate immediately Blast injury Wait 60 sec for scene safety, then triage, then MARCH Traumatic amputation Tourniquet high and tight immediately, then shock treatment Burns Cool with water 1020 min, cover loosely, no home remedies Not breathing (has pulse) Head tilt-chin lift, rescue breaths 1 per 5 seconds No breathing, no pulse 30 chest compressions then 2 breaths, 100 120 per minute Choking (conscious) 5 back blows alternating with 5 abdominal thrusts Tension pneumothorax Release chest seal briefly, evacuate immediately This

Every condition described here requires professional medical care as soon as it is available. Do not delay evacuation to complete field care when evacuation is possible. Joseph Alton, MD I dedicate this book to my husband, Joe, a man dedicated to his mission: to put a medically [Page 2] prepared person in every family for any disaster. Amy Alton, ARNP In addition, we both dedicate this book to those who are ready to serve as medical resources in times of disaster. We salute your courage in accepting this assignment; have no doubt, you will save lives. Joseph Alton, MD, and Amy Alton, ARNP Do what you can, with what you have, where you are. Theodore Roosevelt ABOUT THE AUTHORS Joseph Alton practiced as a board-certified obstetrician and pelvic surgeon for more than twenty- five years before retiring to devote his efforts to preparing families medically for any scenario. He is a fellow of the American College of Obstetrics and Gynecology and the American College of Surgeons, and served as department chairman at local hospitals and as an adjunct professor at local university nursing schools. He is a contributor to well-known preparedness magazines and is frequently invited to speak at survival and preparedness conferences throughout the country. A member of MENSA, Dr. Alton collects medical books from the nineteenth century to gain insight on off-the-grid medical protocols. Amy Alton is an advanced registered nurse practitioner and a certified nurse- midwife. She has had years of experience working in large teaching institutions as well as smaller, family-oriented hospitals. Amy has extensive medicinal herb and vegetable gardens and works to include natural remedies into her gardening strategies. As Dr. Bones and Nurse Amy, these medical professionals host a medical preparedness website at www.doomandbloom.net and produce video and radio programs under the Doom and BloomTM label. Dr. and Ms. Alton are firm believers that, to remain healthy in hard times, we must use all the tools in the medical woodshed. Their goal is to promote integrated medicine; in this way, they can offer their readers the most options to keep their loved ones healthy in a long- term survival situation.
